data.title: Expansion Into Texas Could Boost Glacier Bancorp
data.description:
In August, I wrote an analysis of Seacoast Banking Corporation of Florida (SBCF), referring to the company as a “serial acquirer”. At the time, the holding company for Seacoast Bank had just announced its 17th takeover in the last 11 years, with the Villages Bancorporation being its latest target. While the deal-making pace from Glacier Bancorp (NYSE:GBCI) is not quite up to the high levels set by SBCF, it is certainly getting closer after its June announcement to buy Guaranty Bancshares in an all-stock transaction. The acquisition was Glacier's 27th since 2000 and its 13th in the past decade. The deal for Guaranty closed on September 30.

As part of the press release for the deal, Glacier President and CEO Randy Chesler made the following statement:

We are thrilled to add Guaranty Bank & Trust to the Glacier family of banks as a new banking Division. This is a compelling opportunity to further expand our presence in the Southwest. Guaranty fits strategically and culturally within the unique Glacier business model and will allow us to enter a complementary state with an exceptional demographic profile, strong growth prospects, and a business-friendly operating environment. The Texas economy is estimated to be worth $2.7 trillion, and if Texas were an independent country, its economy would be the 8th largest in the world. This acquisition continues our long history of consistently adding high quality community banks to our proven banking model and we are very enthusiastic about the future opportunities this partnership will provide.

Glacier Bancorp needs all the help it can get after stringing together a rather choppy performance over the last several years. Rising non-interest expenses have coincided with relatively flat revenue growth and declining profits for the last four years. The chart for the company’s stock has reflected this lackluster showing, losing just over three percent of its value since October 7, 2022.

Expansion into a high-growth area like Texas has the potential to be a much-needed boost to Glacier’s bottom line. After acquiring Guaranty Bancshares, Glacier now controls 33 banking locations in 26 cities that include Dallas, Houston, and Austin. However, it will take some time for potential synergies to be revealed and for Glacier to improve its operations in the eight western states in which it already had a presence. I consider GBCI to be a Hold at this time, with several operating metrics falling under a level that would give me more confidence about its prospects going forward.

Company Overview

Glacier Bancorp is the holding company for 18 different regional and community banks with a geographic footprint that now stretches from Texas up to Washington State. At the end of 2024, prior to its Guaranty acquisition, Glacier’s highest concentration of branch locations was found in Montana, Utah, and Idaho. The largest share of its deposits was in Montana (25.9%) and Wyoming (15.8%). A full list of the names and locations for the banks controlled by Glacier Bancorp can be seen below.

GBCI has a market cap of $5.75 billion. Based on the $3.1 billion of reported assets for Guaranty Bancshares on June 30, Glacier Bancorp now has assets of about $32.1 billion. The company holds roughly $24.4 billion in deposits with an impressive 29.6% of that total being non-interest bearing.

In a presentation that listed the details of its Guaranty acquisition, Glacier projected its loan portfolio would be approximately $20.4 billion, post-merger. The portfolio is diversified with no specific loan type holding more than 27.6% of the total book of loans, although two of the top three loan types are commercial real estate. Non-owner occupied commercial real estate and farm loans is the largest allocation with residential real estate and owner occupied commercial real estate being second and third.

GBCI pays an annual regular dividend of $1.32, an amount that has not changed since 2023. At its current share price, the stock has a dividend yield of 2.72%, slightly below its sector median of 2.99%. The company only earned $1.74 per share in 2024, giving its stock a dividend payout ratio of 75.8%, almost twice the media for other regional banks. The company is expected to earn $2.27 this year, which would reduce its payout to 58%. While the dividend does not appear to be currently at risk, the stock receives several low scores from Seeking Alpha's Quant rating system for safety, growth, and yield.

Analysis of Glacier Bancorp

In its Q2 2025 earnings report, Glacier Bancorp reported GAAP earnings of $0.45 per share, $0.07 ahead of expectations. This was $0.03 less than Q1 2025, but $0.06 ahead of Q2 2024. The company has now reported GAAP earnings of $0.93 for the first six months of 2025, $0.25 better than the same period last year. The earnings improvement can be attributed mainly to an increase in the company’s loan yields and a decrease in the cost of funding.

There are several other positives to note beyond just the bottom-line upgrades that have been made in the last year. Net interest margin has been improving as well, reaching 3.21% during Q2 2025. NIM was 3.04% at the end of Q1 2025 and just 2.68% during Q2 2024. The size of this increase, 53 basis points in 12 months, is impressive. The company reported that its net interest income reached $802 million for the first half of this year, an increase of 19.7% from the first half of last year.

Glacier’s efficiency ratio has been improving as well. After averaging just above 71% in the first half of last year, it has fallen to 63.7% during the same period this year. For the second quarter, the ratio was just 62.1%. Non-interest expenses for the company are still elevated, however, because of costs associated with mergers and acquisitions.

The company's return on equity in Q2 of this year, 10.15%, is another metric that has improved significantly since last year. At the end of Q2 2024, ROE for Glacier was just 8.04%. The national average for a regional bank of Glacier's size is 12% according to the FDIC. Return on assets for GBCI has been falling precipitously since the pandemic, but finally showed signs of a rebound this year, rising 21 basis points from Q2 2024 to reach 0.77% in Q2 2025.

My biggest concern for GBCI is its valuation. The stock trades at a PE of 23.22 and a forward PE of 21.48. That number is almost twice the median for its sector. Even the stock’s forward PE for FY 2026 is higher than the current sector median.

Another way to look at valuation for Glacier Bancorp would be to consider its possible buyout price should it be the target of interest from a larger bank. The median price paid for mergers this year, according to S&P Global, is about 1.5 times tangible book value. With Glacier’s last reported tangible book value reported to be $19.79, its stock currently trades at 2.40x its tangible book value, or TBV. Due in large part to its multiple acquisitions, the company carries a great deal of goodwill on its books, with its reported book value at $10.01 more than its TBV.

Risks to Consider

Glacier Bancorp has actually closed two acquisitions this year. The bank holding company added the Bank of Idaho to its portfolio in on May 1. Combined with Guaranty Bancshares, Glacier has now added $4.4 billion in assets to its balance sheet. While both deals may be substantially accretive to Glacier’s bottom line in the long-term, there are a number of risks associated with both of these deals including higher-than-expected costs, operational conflict between management, and customer attrition.

The company is heavily dependent on the real estate market with almost two-thirds of its loan portfolio dedicated to some type of commercial real estate. This could expose Glacier Bancorp to risks with rising default rates, restructuring, and refinancing. Non-performing assets for the company increased $20.8 million in the first six months of 2025 to 0.17% of its assets. While this is still a relatively low number, the ratio for non-performing assets was 0.06% in the first half of 2024. Provision for credit losses also increased in the first half of this year to $28.1 million from just $11.8 million during the same period last year. Much of this increase was attributed to the assets acquired from the Bank of Idaho. Allowances for credit losses as a percentage of bank loans for the first half of this year was 1.22% which, according to Glacier, placed it in the 49th percentile for its peer group, not a great rank, but not a terrible one either.

The Bottom Line

Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating deems GBCI to be a Hold, with the rating of 3.0 being dead center on its scale. For much of the last two quarters, the stock has actually had a Sell rating. There are currently no factors from the quant system that have a score above "C+". The “D+” in Valuation stems from the company’s relatively high PE ratio, PEG ratio, and price-to-sales ratio.

I do see potential for the Guaranty acquisition to be a driver for better overall performance for Glacier as it releases its Q4 earnings report. The Q3 earnings report that will be released next week will be the last one from GBCI without Guaranty included. Guaranty had a number of performance metrics that were better than those from Glacier at the time the deal was announced. The power of the Texas economy gives the Glacier access to several strong economic areas in the Lone Star State. Glacier is expected to announce GAAP earnings of $0.61 per share in Q3.

Analysts that cover GBCI expect the company to earn $3.14 per share in FY 2026. If projections hold true, that will give Glacier earnings growth approaching 40%, but it would still leave the company with a PE ratio above the median for its sector. I consider the stock to be a Hold as there is nothing in my research that gives me a compelling reason to want to be an owner in the company at its current share price.

data.title: Trump Relights The Tariff Fuse
data.description:
Markets rediscovered volatility Friday after President Trump’s latest diplomatic haiku: threaten 100% tariffs, cancel a meeting, then wink that maybe he’ll still go. Traders reacted like toddlers hearing thunder for the first time: loudly and with tears. Of course, for the second time.

Level Change 10/10/25 (%)

-1.9 Dow

-3.6 Nasdaq

-3.5 Nasdaq 100

-2.7 S&P 500

-2.8 S&P 400

-3.2 S&P 600

It was the worst day for stocks since April’s “Liberation Day” selloff, a patriotic name for an unpatriotic event. The S&P 500 fell 2.7%, the Nasdaq 100 dumped 3.5%, and the so-called Magnificent Seven lost 3.8%, proving magnificence to be as unshakeable as a Jell-O skyscraper in an earthquake.

The trigger? President Trump’s post on Truth Social, the platform where policy ideas go to die of exposure. He accused China of being “very hostile” and vowed “a massive increase” in tariffs. Ah yes, the tariff, Trump’s Swiss Army knife for every problem.

Within hours, he elaborated: a 100% levy on Chinese goods and export controls on “any and all critical software,” beginning Nov. 1. (Happy Halloween, supply chain!) He added that he might still meet Xi Jinping - “we’ll see what happens” - statesman code for stuck in traffic, might bail.

The VIX spiked above 20, its first foray into double-digit anxiety since spring. Traders clutching volatility ETFs looked smug for the first time since the March massacre, while bond yields tiptoed lower. A flight to safety? Possibly a pirouette to panic.

Beijing chuckled at how easily it gets the POTUS thumbs a-tappin’.

Middle Kingdom customs officers reportedly blocked shipments of Nvidia (NVDA) chips, regulators opened an antitrust probe into Qualcomm’s (QCOM) Autotalks deal, and someone decided US cargo ships should pay new fees at Chinese ports.

Trump still doesn’t understand that he brought a tariff to a rare-earth fight.

The real blow was Beijing’s sweeping new export controls on rare earths and related technologies, its latest flex in the long game of mineral leverage ahead of the Trump-Xi meeting this month. It’s Beijing’s way of showing the White House what foresight looks like.

Rare earths have been China’s ace from the start, positioned by the country’s patient planners after the first Trump trade tiff back in the twenty-teens. If only the US administration had paused to plan before tweet-starting its new trade travesty.

Tariffs are wind. Rare earths are Earth: physical, real. In trade, the conceptual loses to the tangible.

By afternoon, Wall Street resembled a herd of gazelles realizing the watering hole was a crocodile farm. The closing bell stopped the screaming.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: 6 IPOs Debut, Pipeline Grows, Despite Government Shutdown
data.description:
The US IPO market saw plenty of activity this week, as issuers move to adapt to the ongoing government shutdown. Six IPOs debuted this week, while two SPACs listed. Thirteen IPOs and five SPACs also submitted initial filings.

Commercial washing machine manufacturer Alliance Laundry Holdings (ALH) priced its upsized IPO at the high end of the range to raise $826 million at a $4.5 billion market cap. Acquired by BDT Capital Partners in 2015, the company makes commercial laundry systems used in healthcare facilities, hotels, laundromats, fire stations, and other institutional settings, holding an estimated 40% share of the North American commercial laundry market. Alliance Laundry finished the week up 14%.

Post-secondary education provider Phoenix Education Partners (PXED) priced its IPO at the midpoint to raise $136 million at a $1.3 billion market cap. Through the University of Phoenix, the company provides post-secondary education, primarily to working adults seeking to advance their careers, offering 72 degree-granting and 33 non-degree certificate programs across a wide range of disciplines. Phoenix Education finished the week up 13%.

Chinese online medical platform Pomdoctor (POM) priced its US IPO at the low end of the range to raise $20 million at a $474 million market cap. The company is an online medical services platform for chronic diseases in China. Pomdoctor finished the week up 9%.

Philippines-based recycling firm One and one Green Tech (YDDL) prices its downsized US IPO at the midpoint to raise $10 million at a $270 market cap. One and one is a waste materials and scrap metal recycling company processing raw materials and generating final products that include copper alloy ingot, aluminum scraps, and plastic beads, among others. One and one Green Tech finished the week up 31%.

Japanese sports class provider Leifras (LFS) priced its US IPO at the low end of the range to raise $5 million at a $105 million market cap. Its core business is its children's sports schools, which refers to a series of courses and programs that the company offers to teach a sport instead of a physical location. Leifras finished the week down 5%.

Wound and dermatology-focused Turn Therapeutics (TTRX) completed its direct listing this week. Turn Therapeutics develops products using its proprietary PermaFusion mixing process, which stabilizes water-soluble pharmaceutical ingredients in oil without emulsifiers, and is advancing its FDA-cleared Hexagen formulation for new indications, including eczema and onychomycosis. It finished the week down 26% from its $10 opening price.

Two SPACs also debuted. While not listed below, Avi Katz’s GigCapital8 (GIWWU) raised $220 million last Friday and began trading this week. Lake Superior Acquisition (LKSPU) raised $100 million to target the energy storage, social media, and consumer staples sectors.

7 IPOs During the Week of October 6th, 2025
Issuer Business Deal Size Market Cap at IPO Price vs. Midpoint First Day Return Return at 10/10

Waste materials and scrap metal recycling company in the Philippines.

Developing drugs and medical products for the wound and dermatology markets.

Alliance Laundry (ALH)

World's leading producer of commercial washing machines and dryers.

Offers sports classes for children in Japan.

Lake Superior Acquisition (LKSPU)

 $100M $137M 0% +0% +0%
Blank check company targeting the energy storage, social media, and consumer staples industries.

Thirteen IPOs submitted initial filings, including three sizable issuers. Molecular diagnostics company BillionToOne (BLLN) filed to raise $100 million. Inflammatory disease biotech Evommune (EVMN) filed to raise $100 million. Missouri-based bank Central Bancompany (CBC) filed to raise $100 million. Government relations service provider Public Policy Holding Company (PPHC) filed to raise $60 million. Autonomous lawn care equipment manufacturer FireFly Automatix (FFLY) filed to raise $25 million. Pet biotech Akston Biosciences (AXTN) filed to raise $20 million at a $100 million market cap. Chinese optical module developer Boundless Group (BLTG) filed to raise $18 million at a $198 million market cap. Automotive part distributor Sonic Lighting (SONC) filed to raise $11 million at a $74 million market cap. Malaysian fiber-optic infrastructure provider Neutrans (NEUT) filed to raise $9 million at a $119 million market cap. Hong Kong consulting firm Laws Business Group Holding (LSBA) filed to raise $8 million at a $121 million market cap. Asset manager Keystone Global Financial Group (KCG) filed to raise $6 million at a $63 million market cap. Mineral exploration company Idaho Copper (OTCPK:COPR) filed to raise $5 million at a $123 million market cap in an uplisting. Personal security platform TG-17 (OBAI) filed for a direct listing.

Five SPACs also submitted initial filings. Led by executives from fintech Xsolla, Xsolla SPAC 1 (XSOLU.RC) filed to raise $250 million. Alussa Energy Acquisition II (ALUBU) filed to raise $250 million to target energy and power infrastructure. Soren Acquisition (SORNU) filed to raise $220 million to target healthcare. BOA Acquisition II (THEOU) filed to raise $200 million to target real estate and infrastructure. Asia-focused Peace Acquisition (PECEU) filed to raise $60 million.

18 Filings During the Week of October 6th, 2025

Provides personal security services via a tech-enabled platform.

BOA Acquisition II (THEOU)

 $200M SPAC Cohen & Company
Blank check company targeting real estate and infrastructure.

FireFly Automatix (FFLY)

 $25M Industrials Roth Cap.
Manufacturer of autonomous lawn mowing and agricultural machinery.

Laws Business Group (LSBA)

 $8M Industrials Cathay Securities
Hong Kong-based provider of financing, accounting, and corporate services.

Week Ahead

No traditional IPOs are currently scheduled for the week ahead as the government shutdown continues to mostly pause new issuance. However, as we saw this past week, SPACs and micro-caps can still price their deals with little notice. In addition, pre-revenue solar electric vehicle developer Aptera Motors (SEV) plans to complete a direct listing on the Nasdaq on Thursday, October 16.

Right now, companies can still go public if they have already had their registration statements declared effective by the SEC, which is the case for many smaller names on file. The majority of notable IPO candidates will likely need to wait or opt for a more creative alternative, like the rarely-used pathway under Section 8(a) of the Securities Act. This allows registration statements to automatically become effective after 20 days, provided the company launches with a proposed price rather than a range.

However, in new guidance issued by the SEC this week, companies can now rely on Rule 430A in addition to Section 8(a), allowing them to launch IPOs with a price range as well as price outside of the range. The 20-day waiting period for effectiveness still applies. This increased flexibility may encourage more companies to move forward with listings if the shutdown extends much longer.

Street research is expected for five companies in the week ahead, and three lock-up periods will be expiring.

IPO Market Snapshot

The Renaissance IPO Indices are market cap-weighted baskets of newly public companies. As of 10/9/2025, the Renaissance IPO Index was up 20.5% year-to-date, while the S&P 500 was up 15.7%. Renaissance Capital's IPO ETF (IPO) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Astera Labs (ALAB) and Arm (ARM). The Renaissance International IPO Index was up 44.1% year-to-date, while the ACWX was up 28.2%. Renaissance Capital’s International IPO ETF (IPOS) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Galderma and Horizon Robotics.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Six Reasons To Ride Apple's Rally Cautiously And Why Continue To Use APLY
data.description:
In June 2025, I did not have a strong directional view on Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL). I ended up recommending the YieldMax AAPL Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:APLY), a conservative call writing variant of AAPL. The defensive positioning has not hurt us by much. Since the time of writing, APLY has been able to capture a significant ~20% of a ~27% rally in AAPL. That is great performance by a defensive covered call strategy, providing the psychological cushion of drawdown risks. I would not have been able to enter AAPL at the time for fear of heights and several supply chain, fundamental, and geopolitical risks staring me in the face after what I thought was an overdone rally.

APLY has given me a way to earn income and ride AAPL at the highs, even when I was wrong. Being defensive was not the wrong call though - at least it enabled an entry and hence an ability to ride the subsequent journey to $250. I see more of the same advice holding true after a ~27% further rally in AAPL - go defensive if you have to ride AAPL. APLY will enable decent upside capture if further momentum led gains transpire. It can do relatively okay in consolidations too. If there is a correction, APLY cuts losses by a few percentage points and helps enter AAPL a few dollars cheaper (saved from the drawdown mitigation).

Three Reasons Why Apple is not a Short

Apple's resilience in what is an unsupportive tariff environment is visible in its free cash flow margin - under pressure in the past few quarters, but not budging below ~23.5%. While the trend is worrying, the degree of impact is much lower than what many other companies would have managed with the degree of tariff impacts like AAPL has. The profit margins, too, have remained stable, through a period of initial tariff repercussions. The tariff story is far from being over, but the initial margin resilience does show Apple's durable pricing power, operational efficiencies, and financial resilience to weather short-term operational and market pressures. The range of margin fluctuations is extremely tight for a company of Apple's size. A drop from ~27.5% to ~23.5% can be passed off as mere fluctuations if it had been any other company - at Apple, this drop corresponds to a once-in-a-decade tariff environment.

Apple has beaten EPS expectations in 11 out of the past 12 quarters. The last quarterly results were a beat on both revenues and EPS. There is a higher probability of a beat in the upcoming earnings session at the end of the month than not, simply going by history. EPS is expected to grow by 12% sequentially and almost 80% YoY (the latter due to a poorer comparable in September 2024). The topline is also expected to grow by ~8% YoY, primarily on the back of the iPhone 16 Series launch. There are other places where topline momentum appears strong too - the M4 powered Macs, Wearables, App Store subscriptions, and even financial and advertising services. Even if working capital remains elevated, I expect operating cash flow to hold near current levels, buoyed by sales and new launches.

Profit margins are also unlikely to be impacted significantly due to a mix of services expansion, pricing power, and decent cost control. In fact, healthy service revenue expansion is the second reason why I can't short Apple. In Q3 2025, services revenues were up ~13%, reaching a record annualized rate of ~$109b. Services and subscriptions growth is needless to say higher margin growth. With a massive installed device base, Apple has huge potential to monetize services revenue further, unlocking margin expansion.

Thirdly, the tariff impacts are looking manageable in terms of the quantum of impact, relative to Apple's operational scale. In Q3, Apple reported a ~$800m impact, not far from the projected $1.1b in Q4. Despite this heavy near peak tariff impact, given Q4 numbers are not very far off, the gross margins were reported at around 46.5%, a 60 basis point sequential decline due to tariffs. Even the peak of tariff impact does not seem to be material enough to impact Apple's margin profile by much.

Three Reasons Why Apple is not a Buy Either

Despite the resilient Apple story, buying Apple now requires lots of courage, particularly for value investors. When it was trading at around $200, the valuations of around ~27x on a forward PE basis had looked expensive given uncertain supply chain and geopolitical risks. Current valuations of ~32x price to forward earnings are near Apple's own historical highs - about 30% higher than Apple's 5-year average.

The second reason why Apple looks not primed for a Buy is its falling market share and competitive positioning, particularly in China. Apple's market share plunged to its all time low in June 2025. iPhone 16 launch could revive some of the market share temporarily, but the systemic challenge from Chinese competitors like Xiaomi, Oppo and Vivo is hard to ignore. If Apple's premium positioning is not upheld, the entire margin story that revolves around that will come under pressure.

Thirdly, I don't find Apple's AI story as compelling as Google and Microsoft, while its premium valuations don't acknowledge the widening gap there. Apple is behind the curve here as well, by a few years. While Google had TPUs since 2016, Apple is setting up AI infrastructure now, a multiyear process and a lag compared to the rest of big tech. A privacy first approach also makes training data difficult as data collection is limited.

The APLY Strategy Yet Again

To understand what APLY brings to the table in an uncertain phase for Apple i.e. neither a Sell nor a Buy, we have already seen its ability to capture ~20% upside in a rally of about ~25% in the underlying. What happens if the underlying stays rangebound? Since such a move often means a few ups and downs - the overall gains in APLY and covered call strategies in general remain subdued. Historically, you can see APLY broadly traces AAPL in such a scenario. From July 2024 to August 2025, for more than a year of end-to-end consolidation, APLY was not any better or worse off.

APLY does show its value by reducing drawdowns by a couple of percentage points - not a huge difference, but enables an entry into AAPL with 2-3 percentage points of savings, even if a 33% correction plays out. Of course, APLY's savings remain limited and constant i.e. 2-3 percentage point range irrespective of the depth of the fall. So, APLY looks like a better proposition in shorter falls. If APPL falls by 10%, APLY may fall by ~7%, looking like a decent strategy to switch to AAPL at lower levels (a discounted entry).

APLY fits into the scheme of things specifically now, as it did in June. This is because entering Apple at the highs is difficult when fundamentals are not strong enough. There are competitive and valuation pressures, and I do not find Apple's AI strategy as evolved enough to demand the valuations it does - any challenge to its pricing power and brand positioning could erode the premium valuations very fast. At the same time, I do not see a deep correction either - numbers are strong, and the tariff repercussions have been handled well so far. As I see AAPL consolidate over the next few quarters, APLY should be able to trace much of that. If AAPL rallies from here, I will be fine with letting go of 4-5 percentage points for the insurance APLY provides in the case of a 5-10% correction.

All in All, AAPL continues to be a Hold and APLY a Buy. The biggest strength in APLY's thesis is that it gives an opportunity to enter AAPL with reduced risks. That insurance helps overcome fear of heights and enables relative wins in all scenarios. It grows when AAPL grows, although more slowly. It gives a more favorable entry point (2-3 percentage point cheaper) if AAPL corrects. APLY's aggressive yield also allows for dynamic decision-making as the AAPL story evolves. Investors can choose to redeploy the payouts in AAPL if the going is strong or a favorable entry point is found in Apple, and deploy the payouts elsewhere if the story consolidates or loses steam.

data.title: June World And Non-OPEC Oil Production Rise
data.description:
A guest post by Ovi

The focus of this post is an overview of World oil production along with a more detailed review of the top 11 Non-OPEC oil-producing countries. OPEC production is covered in a separate post.

Below are a number of Crude plus Condensate (C + C) production charts, usually shortened to “oil,” for the oil-producing countries. The charts are created from data provided by the EIA’s International Energy Statistics and are updated to June 2025. This is the latest and most detailed/complete World oil production information available. Information from other sources such as OPEC, the STEO and country-specific sites such as Brazil, Norway, Mexico, Argentina and China is used to provide a short-term outlook.

The World’s June oil production increased by 895 kb/d to 84,184 kb/d, green graph. Last month’s projection for June was 84,117 kb/d, short by 67 kb/d.

This chart also projects World C + C production out to December 2026. It uses the October 2025 STEO report along with the International Energy Statistics to make the projection. Production in July is projected to increase by 553 kb/d to 84,737 kb/d, if correct, a new World Peak Oil. The 12-month CMA plotted at July 2026 is 85,314 kb/d.

For December 2026, production is projected to be 85,538 b/d, an upward revision of 853 kb/d from last month. Also, September 2025 production is projected to be close to 86,875 kb/d another new high. Will September 2025 be the World’s New Peak Oil date?

What are the chances that the July increase of 553 kb/d will push World oil production to a new Peak Oil? Below are the July increases that I have obtained from the oil reporting agencies of the countries shown. They are typically correct to ± 5 kb/d. The OPEC+ increase came from the September MOMR. Not included is the 109 kb/d US July increase. With a potential 873 kb/d increase in July production, it may exceed November 2018.

June’s World oil output without the US increased by 809 kb/d to 70,651 kb/d. July’s production is expected to increase by 445 kb/d to 71,096 kb/d.

The STEO is forecasting that December 2026 crude output will be 72,024 kb/d, 528 kb/d higher than projected last month. Production will peak in September at 73,613 kb/d.

World oil production W/O the US from July 2025 to December 2026 is forecast to increase by a total of 928 kb/d.

A Different Perspective on World Oil Production

June’s Big 3 oil production increased by 748 kb/d to 33,283 kb/d. The US and Saudi Arabia contributed 783 kb/d to the increase. Russia dropped 35 kb/d. OPEC started increasing production in April 2025 and will continue doing so up to December 2026, so the increase should continue.

Production in the Remaining countries has been slowly increasing since the September 2020 low of 42,970 kb/d to December 2023. Production in June 2025 rose by 148 kb/d to 50,901 kb/d.

Countries Expected to Grow Oil Production

This chart was first posted a number of months back and shows the combined oil production from five Non-OPEC countries, Argentina, Brazil, Canada, Guyana and the US, whose oil production is expected to grow. These five countries are often cited by OPEC and the IEA for being capable of meeting the increasing World oil demand for next year. For these five countries, production from April 2020 to August 2024 rose at an average rate of 1,199 kb/d/year, as shown by the orange OLS line.

To show the impact of US growth over the past 5 years, US production was removed from the five countries, and that graph is shown in red. The production growth slope for the remaining four countries has been reduced by 687 kb/d/yr to 512 kb/d/yr.

June production has been added to the five growers chart, up by 473 kb/d to 23,437 kb/d. For the five growers W/O US, June production rose to 9,857 kb/d, up 340 kb/d from April.

The OLS lines have not been updated and will not be updated going forward unless additional production data provides a strong indication that production is rising/changing significantly. However, looking out to July, production from Brazil will rise by 200 kb/d (see Brazil chart below). Combining this with an expected rebound from Canada and rising production from Argentina, new highs from these five can be expected.

World Oil Countries Ranked by Production

Above are listed the World’s 13th-largest oil-producing countries. In June 2025, these 13 countries produced 79.1% of the World’s oil. On a MoM basis, production increased by 1,089 kb/d in these 13 countries, while on a YOY basis, production rose by 2,258 kb/d.

June Non-OPEC Oil Production Charts

June’s Non-OPEC oil production increased by 468 kb/d to 53,839 kb/d. July is expected to add an additional 747 kb/d to 54,586 kb/d.

Using data from the October 2025 STEO, a projection for Non-OPEC oil output was made for the period June 2025 to December 2026. (Red graph). Output is expected to reach 55,613 kb/d in December 2026.

From July 2025 to December 2026, oil production in Non-OPEC countries is expected to increase by 1,027 kb/d.

June’s Non-OPEC W/O US oil production increased by 381 kb/d to 40,306 kb/d. July’s production is projected to add 639 kb/d to 40,945 kb/d.

From July 2025 to December 2026, production in Non-OPEC countries W/O the US is expected to increase by 1,154 to 42,099 kb/d.

Non-OPEC Oil Countries Ranked by Production

Listed above are the World’s 11 largest Non-OPEC producers. The original criteria for inclusion in the table was that all of the countries produced more than 1,000 kb/d. Oman has recently fallen below 1,000 kb/d, while Angola had a big drop in June.

June’s MoM production increased by 595 kb/d to 45,174 kb/d for these eleven Non-OPEC countries, while as a whole, the Non-OPEC countries saw a yearly production increase of 1,258 kb/d to 53,839 kb/d.

In June 2025, these 11 countries produced 83.5% of all Non-OPEC oil.

Non-OPEC Country’s Oil Production Charts

Angola’s June production dropped 100 kb/d to 905 kb/d. Angola’s production has been dropping slowly since January 2024, but June had a big drop to 905 kb/d. While the EIA is reporting June production was below 1,000 kb/d, this Article claims July was the first month in which production dropped below 1,000 kb/d.

“Angola’s oil production for July was 30,961,452 barrels, corresponding to a daily average of 998,757 barrels of oil (BOPD) against a forecast of 1,073,542 BOPD,” reads the website of the National Oil, Gas and Biofuels Agency, the Angolan regulator of this sector.

The EIA reported that Brazil’s June production increased by 78 kb/d to 3,757 kb/d.

Brazil’s National Petroleum Association (BNPA) reported that production increased by 199 kb/d in July to 3,956 kb/d, a new high, and then dropped 65 kb/d in August. The pre-salt graph tracks Brazil’s trend in the blue graph. Pre-salt production increased by 167 kb/d in July to 3,148 kb/d, also a new high.

The September OPEC report states July crude production was driven by strong production from recently started projects.

Canada’s production increased by 424 kb/d in June to 4,848 kb/d. The production rise in June was related to the end of maintenance. A projection has been made for July production based on preliminary production from the Canada Energy Regulator. July production could reach a new high of 5,115 kb/d.

According to this Article, the Canadian oil and gas industry is anticipating modest production growth in 2026.

"About 88pc of oil and gas producers expect to grow production in the next 12 months, up from 79pc saying so in the bank’s previous survey released in April. Gas producers are expecting growth of about 6.3pc while oil producers see about 4.6pc growth, with that divergence between commodities further amplified in company’s capital spending plans. Gas-weighted producers plan to increase capital expenditures by 5.2pc in 2026, compared with a 0.4pc decline seen among oil-weighted producers.“

According to this magazine Article, In-Situ oil production is outpacing mining. Also, another article says oil exports to countries other than the US are slowing.

The EIA reported China’s June oil output rose by 80 kb/d to 4,430 kb/d.

The China National Bureau of Statistics reported July production dropped to 4,268 kb/d and then rose to 4,299 kb/d in August.

On a YoY basis, China’s June production increased by 180 kb/d from 4,250 kb/d.

According to the EIA, Kazakhstan’s June oil output increased by 125 kb/d to 2,146 kb/d.

Kazakhstan’s recent pre-salt crude oil production, as reported by Argus, was added to the chart. In October 2024, pre-salt crude production dropped by 120 kb/d to a low 1,340 kb/d. Since then, production to June 2025 has risen by 464 kb/d. The production ramp-up starting in January 2025 is due to a New Field coming online. Note this is crude production, whereas the EIA’s numbers are C + C. S&P Global Platts reports that July production was essentially flat.

According to the EIA, Mexico’s June output dropped by 6 kb/d to 1,719 kb/d.

In June 2024, Pemex issued a new and modified oil production report for Heavy, Light and Extra Light oil. It is shown in blue in the chart, and it appears that Mexico is not reporting condensate production when compared to the EIA report.

In earlier EIA reports, they would add close to 55 kb/d of condensate to Pemex’s “Total Liquids” report. More recently, the EIA has been adding 90 kb/d of condensate to Mexican production. For July and August production, 90 kb/d have been added to estimate Mexico’s July and August C + C production, red markers. July production is estimated to be close to 1,738 kb/d while August dropped to 1,735. Note that Mexico’s production for the last five months has stopped dropping and has stabilized around 1,650 kb/d, according to Pemex.

The EIA reported Norway’s June production dropped by 110 kb/d to 1,686 kb/d.

Separately, the Norway Petroleum Directorate (NPD) reported that July’s production rose by 280 kb/d to 1,966, a post-pandemic new high, red markers. August production dropped to 1,937 kb/d.

The Norway Petroleum Directorship reported that August’s oil production was 7.1% above forecast.

According to the July OPEC MOMR: "Production at the Johan Castberg FPSO, the main driver of crude oil production growth this year, reached peak capacity of 220 tb/d in late June, according to operator Equinor. This ramp-up took less than three months from project start-up."

Oman’s production rose very consistently since the low of May 2020. However, production began to drop in November 2022. According to the EIA, June’s output rose by 6 kb/d to 997 kb/d and appears to be stabilizing in the 980 kb/d to 1,000 kb/d range.

The EIA had been reporting flat output of 1,322 kb/d for Qatar since early 2022. However, the EIA revised down all of the previous production data up to April 2024. Qatar’s June output was again reported to be 1,322 kb/d.

The EIA reported Russia’s June 2025 C + C production decreased by 35 kb/d to 9,783 kb/d and was up 19 kb/d from June 2024.

Ukraine has bombed Russian refineries sporadically over the past few years. However, beginning around mid-year 2025, the intensity of refinery attacks increased, and more specifically, the drones have gone after the distillation towers. This has resulted in gasoline shortages in Russia. Also, they have attacked export terminals and pipelines. It is not clear when the effects of these attacks will show up in declining crude oil production. Note the EIA is showing dropping production in May and June, which is opposite to the Argus data.

Using data from Argus Media reports, Russian crude production is shown in the blue graph. For June 2025, Argus reported Russian crude production was 9,020 kb/d. Adding 8% to Argus' June crude production provides a C + C production estimate of 9,742 kb/d for Russia, which is a proxy for the Pre-War Russian Ministry estimate, red markers. Argus has stopped reporting World oil production. I will check if another source can be used as a cross-check on trends rather than specific production.

According to this Article, Russia’s Crude Output was below the OPEC+ target in August.

The nation pumped 9.175 million barrels a day last month, according to people who saw the data and asked for anonymity, as the information isn’t public. That’s an increase from July, but about 84,000 barrels a day below the country’s quota for August, including compensation cuts, Bloomberg calculations show.

OPEC published an updated compensation schedule on Monday, with Russia pledging to reduce its output in August by 85,000 barrels a day below its target of 9.344 million barrels a day. That brings required production to 9.259 million barrels a day. This required production is 239 kb/d higher than June 2025 production of 9,020 kb/d.

This US production chart up to July 2025 is the same as the one published last week in the US update post. However, the projected portions of the two production graphs have been updated using the October 2025 STEO.

US August projected production has been revised up by close to 147 kb/d to 13,678 kb/d.

Production in December 2026 is expected to be 13,514 kb/d, 314 kb/d higher than shown in last week’s US update. Production peaks in September 2025 and then begins a slow decline. Note production in the Onshore L48 drops steadily after July 2025.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: S&P 500 Snapshot: Largest Loss In 6 Months
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The S&P 500 notched two new record highs this week before experiencing its largest daily loss in six months. The index sank -2.7% on Friday, ultimately leading to a weekly loss of -2.4%. Here is a snapshot of the index from the past week:

The table below summarizes the number of record highs reached each year dating back to 2013.

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past six months with a 50-day moving average:

S&P 500: A Perspective on Drawdowns

On October 9, 2007, the S&P 500 reached an all-time high, closing the day at 1565.15. Then on March 9, 2009, the index dropped ~57% off of its high from exactly 17 months before, closing the day at 676.53. This time period became known as the Global Financial Crisis. It took over 5 years before the index reached a new all-time high on March 28, 2013, where it closed out at 1569.19. The chart below is a snapshot of record highs and selloffs since the 2007 peak reached on October 9, 2007.

What happens if we take out the Global Financial Crisis? Here's a snapshot of the same chart above where the start date has been changed to the trough reached on March 9, 2009. Note the recent selloffs in 2022.

Here are a few tables with the number of days of a 1% or greater change in either direction and the number of days of corrections (down 10% or more from the record high).

And here is a linear chart of the index since October 9, 2007:

Here is a linearly scaled version of the same chart with the 50- and 200-day moving averages. The index has been above the 50-day moving average since May 1st and above the 200-day moving average since May 12th. Additionally, the 50-day moving average has been above the 200-day moving average since July 1st.

S&P 500: A Perspective on Volatility

For a sense of the correlation between the closing price and intraday volatility, the chart below overlays the S&P 500 since 2007 with the intraday price range. On April 9th, the index experienced its largest intraday price volatility (10.77%) since December 24th, 2018 (19.10%). Also included is the 20-day moving average to identify trends in volatility. Over the past 20 days, the average percent change from the intraday low to the intraday high is 0.83%.

S&P 500 versus S&P Equal Weight

The S&P 500 is a market cap-weighted index, which includes roughly the 500 largest U.S. stocks spanning 11 sectors. The S&P 500 Equal Weight Index includes the same constituents as the S&P 500, but each company is equally weighted at a fixed weight. So how do these two indexes match up against each other this year?

The S&P 500 is currently up 11.65% year-to-date, while the S&P Equal Weight is up 5.96% year-to-date.

ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include: iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPLG), and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Byrna Technologies: Q3 Sent Strong Growth Signals
data.description:
Byrna Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:BYRN) reported strong fiscal Q3 results from the June-August period. The less-lethal self defense launcher company continues to show strong momentum, driven by e-commerce and wholesale retail growth. The stock reacted very strongly to Byrna’s strong growth signals in the report, ending the day up by 21%.

I initiated the stock at a Hold rating in my initial August article, titled “Byrna Technologies: Aiming To Capture A Vast Market, But Success Isn't A Given”. The stock has since returned 42%, well above the S&P 500’s 4% return.

Byrna’s Q3 Sent Strong Growth Signals

Byrna’s Q3 report was strong. Revenues came in at $28.2 million, up by 35% year-on-year through very fast wholesale retail expansion. Growth in Byrna’s own retail channel, international sales, and direct-to-consumer ecommerce also came in at a good level, as the growth story is progressing well. The revenue momentum itself isn’t a reason for Byrna’s stock surge - the revenue figure was already known by the market, as Byrna previously already gave its preliminary Q3 revenues in early September. The topline figure also didn’t show much cause for celebration on a sequential basis, even showing a slight decrease.

Rather, the report sent strong signals of Byrna’s upcoming growth, setting up higher future expectations for Q4 and FY2026. The company previously anticipated 25-40% growth but now guides more enthusiastic 35-40% revenue growth for FY2025 – Q4 revenues are now implied at $35.0 million at the guidance mid-point.

Most importantly, in my opinion, Byrna’s report and earnings call remarks showed very strong signs of upcoming ecommerce growth. Byrna’s own web traffic has improved to average daily web sessions of 58 thousand in September, up from just 33 thousand prior to new advertising campaigns. The momentum has sustained with several days of 70 thousand daily web sessions in October. The Amazon ecommerce channel has followed with similar momentum.

While web growth came in at just 8% in Q3 with web revenues of $16.3 million, improving web conversion rates are seen to drive much faster growth in Q4 and beyond - ecommerce revenue momentum is clearly only starting to build. Byrna’s Semrush statistics also clearly show a continued surge in traffic. Byrna’s “We Don’t Sell Bananas” AI ad has clearly succeeded, and the company is planning ambitious AI and influencer advertising campaigns ahead.

Byrna is now in over 1000 retail locations over the U.S., driving most of the revenue momentum seen in Q3. Yet, as Byrna doesn’t want to become overly saturated, CFO Laurilee Kearnes told in the earnings call that Byrna will probably mostly be holding where Byrna is on its current retail footprint – Byrna is becoming more selective about additional retail partnerships. The revenue stream may not show as rapid growth going forward after the wholesale retail channel has already expanded very quickly.

Adding more SKUs to existing stores and improving brand visibility should still continue to drive growth in retail; Byrna noted significant opportunities within the existing store footprint. Underlining the notation, Sportsman’s Warehouse (SPWH) noted that the retailer has a lot of upside with Byrna and other less-lethal self-defense options in its latest earnings call.

Profitability Is Following Byrna’s Growth

The second main positive in the Q3 report was Byrna’s profitability – adjusted EBITDA came in at $3.7 million, up by 91.5% year-on-year at an already strong 13.2% margin. The adjusted EPS of $0.12 beat Wall Street’s consensus by $0.07.

The gross margin even temporarily declined due to a larger mix of dealer sales and startup costs related to the Compact Launcher, but operating leverage still provided a platform for significant margin gains during the quarter. Byrna has already proven its business’ profitability despite still growing rapidly. Further growth should enable more margin expansion through operating leverage in production and other functions.

BYRN’s Valuation Doesn’t Provide Base Scenario Upside

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Byrna. I now estimate the company to reach $612.5 million in FY2034 revenues, 3.5% of Byrna’s estimate for the company’s addressable market. I previously assumed a 3.0% market share. The estimates now reflect a rapid FY2024-FY2034 CAGR of 21.7%.

I still expect Byrna to reach a 15% EBIT margin, but now by FY2028 already. I still factor in a 15 exit EV/EBIT multiple, and that Byrna converts after-tax earnings relatively well into free cash flow.

The estimates put Byrna’s fair value at $29.1, near the stock price at the time of writing. While Byrna’s momentum stands strong, much is already priced in – I believe that investors should likely wait for a better entry point. A 3.5% market share of Byrna’s very rough $17.5 billion TAM estimate is already priced in.

The fair value estimate is up from $24.7 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 11.90%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I don’t assume Byrna to draw debt. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.11% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate quite high at 1.60. With a liquidity premium of 0.4%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 11.90%.

Takeaway

Byrna’s growth story stands strong as e-commerce momentum is picking up and retail expansion has progressed well. Profitability has followed growing revenues. Yet, as the valuation already prices in significant growth, I believe that investors are likely better off waiting for a better entry point. I remain neutral at a Hold rating for BYRN stock.

data.title: Did Gold And Silver Just Peak? (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Jesse Colombo

After hitting one new high after another, gold, silver, and mining stocks took a breather on Thursday. Gold slipped 1.62% to close at $3,976.24, while silver managed to finish up 1.05% at $49.39, though still well below its earlier intraday high of $51.24.

None of this should come as a surprise or cause for alarm. No asset moves straight up forever. In fact, in my recent writings, I have repeatedly noted that, despite my strong bullish outlook for the broader precious metals bull market, gold and silver have become overheated in the short term.

A shallow pullback or period of sideways consolidation here would be both natural and healthy, allowing them to cool off, reset their overbought conditions, and build the base needed for even greater gains ahead.

On October 3, I said that silver was likely to pause just below its critical $50 psychological level. Rather than crashing, I expected a healthy, shallow pullback or brief consolidation that would allow it to gather strength before ultimately breaking through the $50 level that capped its rallies in 1980 and 2011.

Here’s a relevant excerpt from that piece:

Now I want to say that I adamantly believe silver is going to surpass $50 and go much higher from there in the near future. However, there is something I want to point out. Because silver has surged so strongly and so quickly, it is a bit extended in the short term, which makes it likely that it will consolidate or pause before eventually breaking through $50.

Evidence of this can be seen in the Relative Strength Index (RSI) momentum indicator, shown in the chart below, which provides overbought or oversold readings for assets. I respect what it indicates.

That being said, unlike many amateur investors and analysts, I am adamant that an overbought reading like the current one in silver is not an automatic signal to sell or an indication that the bull market is over.

There is a very high likelihood that silver will experience a shallow pullback or move sideways for a time to work off its overbought condition. This would conserve energy for the next leg higher, when silver smashes through the $50 ceiling.

In that piece, I showed the long-term chart of silver and explained the importance of the $50 level, which capped the rallies in both 1980 and 2011 and led to sharp declines afterward.

Once that level came back into play recently, I pointed out that it would be critical to watch how silver reacts there and that a brief pause or hiccup was likely before it ultimately breaks through (and I expect it to break through this time around).

Now let’s take a look at the daily chart to see how silver behaved today at that critical $50 resistance level. Early in the New York session, it made a sharp attempt to test that level but failed to close above it and then pulled back.

I’m not at all surprised by this since it’s exactly the scenario I was expecting. Everything is unfolding according to the playbook I’ve been following, which is reassuring.

I always prefer when the market behaves as I had anticipated rather than catching me off guard because it reaffirms that I have a solid understanding of the mechanics of the market.

Next, let’s look at the intraday chart so you can see how silver almost exactly touched the $50 level before failing there and selling off about $3 an ounce over the next few hours. That is a textbook example of how key technical levels work and why everyone should pay attention to them.

Those who focus only on fundamentals are living in an ivory tower and are completely out of touch with how the markets work in the real world. Without knowing and following the technicals, it is like flying blind.

Next, I want to show why silver is likely to experience a healthy, shallow pullback or a period of sideways consolidation to work off its overbought condition.

The first chart compares how stretched silver has recently become relative to its 200-day moving average. As you can see, the last few times this occurred, pullbacks or periods of consolidation followed.

However, I do not expect a strong pullback this time because we are still in the early stages of a powerful precious metals bull market, and it is normal to see pauses along the way.

While a brief consolidation may occur, I am not worried at all because silver remains in a confirmed uptrend, and the long-term trend is not at risk. This only refers to short-term market behavior.

Further confirmation that silver has become a bit overheated in the short term can be seen in the Relative Strength Index (RSI), a widely used momentum oscillator that helps identify whether an asset is overbought, oversold, or neutral.

On silver’s daily chart, the RSI recently entered overbought territory. As a reminder, an overbought condition can be worked off through a period of sideways movement; a sharp pullback is not required for that to happen.

Next, let’s look at gold. Just a couple of days ago, everyone was celebrating gold’s move above the critical $4,000 psychological resistance level. But with today’s pullback, it slipped back below that level in both COMEX futures and spot, which makes me pause and think, as it indicates a rejection of that key level.

This increases the likelihood of a modest pullback or a period of sideways consolidation, as gold may have temporarily run out of momentum to push higher in the short term.

That is perfectly normal, healthy, and not at all surprising. It is much like a sprinter who has just run hard and needs a moment to catch their breath.

Like silver, gold became overbought during its powerful surge since late August and has grown extended relative to its 200-day moving average. This indicates that it needs a brief pause to catch its breath before it can resume its upward sprint.

Gold’s Relative Strength Index (RSI) is sending the same message as silver’s, indicating that it is overbought in the short term.

Anyway, to summarize, there is a high likelihood that after their red-hot performances, gold, silver, and mining stocks are due for a healthy short-term pause to catch their breath. I do not expect it to last very long, nor do I believe it will do any damage to the underlying long-term secular bull market.

How am I playing this? Am I dumping my precious metals holdings in a panic? Absolutely not. That could not be further from the truth. I am staying put, still aligned with the uptrend, and doing nothing different.

In fact, yesterday's pullback made me happy because I do not want to see precious metals overheat. I actually welcome a cooldown period because it gives me the opportunity to accumulate more. As an entrepreneur with a rapidly growing income, I want to keep channeling that income into additional precious metals before they take off for the moon.

I do not think many investors are truly ready for the precious metals train to leave the station for good, or for what that would mean for the global economy and world affairs.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Carter's: Oversold With Potential Catalysts
data.description:
Shares of Carter’s (NYSE:CRI) have been a very poor performer over the past year, losing half of their value as the company has struggled with a muted consumer spending environment. While the economy, as a whole, has weathered the significant rise in tariff rates rather well thus far, Carter’s is an example of how tariff policy is having a meaningful impact on some companies and sectors, which are heavily reliant on imported goods with limited pricing power. While the business has faced significant challenges, the sharp decline in the stock may make Carter’s interesting, especially given recent developments.

The baby category is challenged

Carter’s is a leading baby apparel retailer with over 800 locations. The US accounts for about 85% of the business. CRI primarily operates out of its own stores, though it also has a wholesale channel through department stores. This segment has been challenged, as those retailers very closely manage their inventory after struggling with excess inventory for two years. While current management is rightly focused on regaining market share, I see category growth as limited.

Carter’s business faces a simple demographic challenge: we are having fewer babies. It has been well documented that the US, and the developed world as a whole, is seeing its birth rate steadily decline. Births are down about 15% from ~20 years ago. They have plateaued in recent years at the 3.6-3.7 million level. Thus far in 2025, we are on track for ~3.7 million births, comparable to recent years. All else equal, a lower birth rate decreases demand for baby apparel.

As of now, I see no evidence or reason to believe that there will be a reversal of this trend, and this demographic reality simply limits how much the baby category can grow. I also believe that baby-focused retailers are more exposed to demographic challenges than retailers in general. Over time, the US has been able to grow its population as immigration flows offset fewer births. However, while bringing in a 22-year old on a visa can keep the adult population flat and support demand for adult apparel, baby apparel demand does not get a benefit from immigration to the same degree.

Tariffs are squeezing margins

At the same time, tariffs have exacerbated challenges, with recent financials being very weak. In the company’s second quarter, reported back on July 25th, Carter’s earned $0.17, which was $0.20 below expectations even as revenue grew 4% to $585 million. Operating margins shrunk 500 bps to just 2%, even as baby sales were up 10% in Q2, and the company's focus on regaining lost market share in the baby apparel category is showing some progress. Margins are facing a massive squeeze.

As you can see below, Carter is a major importer with significant exposure to Vietnam and Cambodia, leaving it with 20+% tariff costs, while it also has some exposure to China. Management expects about $125-150 million in tariff costs. The H2 impact on pre-tax income will be about $35 million, as it still has some inventory to sell from when tariffs were temporarily 10%.

Management expects to offset these tariff costs in 2026 and beyond, given pricing action, wholesale price cuts, and moving production to lower-tariff countries. Roughly speaking, CRI would need to raise prices by ~4-5% across all products to fully offset tariff costs. I am skeptical of the ability to do that without also causing an impact on volumes, and I expect structural tariff costs of at least $50 million per year.

Frankly, there is limited evidence that CRI can raise prices to this extent. Last quarter, units were up 6%, with pricing & mix a 2% headwind. CRI has been cutting prices to support demand; switching to price increases is likely to be difficult. Because of these pricing actions, margins face significant pressure. While sales were up $21 million from last year, gross profit actually fell by $1 million as margins compressed by 200 bps. At the same time, SG&A grew by 10% to $273 million, causing 290 bps of margin compression. I expect to see increased focus on managing SG&A as a way to offset tariff costs.

This pressure on margins is likely to intensify as CRI begins facing higher-cost inventory. Inventories are up 3% from last year to $619 million. This is in line with sales growth. Notably, the number of units in inventory is down 1%, but the cost is up 4%, primarily due to tariffs. With the cost per unit rising but CRI’s average selling price falling, the margin crunch is likely going to get worse.

Carter's adds a poison pill

While results have been weak, corporate intrigue has risen. Given the weakness in the share price, in late September, Carter’s announced a “temporary stockholder rights plan,” which is commonly known as a poison pill. This maneuver essentially limits the ability of any investor to take more than a 20% stake in CRI. If an investor buys more than 15-20%, CRI will sell shares at a steep discount to all other shareholders to dilute that investor back below 15%. This action occurred after a relatively small hedge fund with $1.3 billion in assets, Roseman Wagner Wealth Management, acquired a ~17% stake in the company.

This action functionally prohibits RWWM from adding to its position, and thus far, there has been no communication between CRI and RWWM. As such, RWWM’s intentions are unknown. It may be seeking to just be a passive investor. It could be tactically investing for a short squeeze (~11% of CRI shares are short). Conversely, it could be planning a proxy fight or hostile bid. The poison pill effectively puts the situation at a standstill until we see what RWWM does next.

I generally look at poison pills negatively. They clearly, in the short term, protect the status quo, which oftentimes protects management more than shareholders. While labelled a shareholder rights plan, a poison pill functionally blocks existing shareholders from selling their shares to a potential buyer of stock. In that sense, poison pills can infringe upon a shareholder’s right. I would like to see this action lead to some publicly documented interactions with RWWM, but for now, we wait to see what the next action will be. I do not believe CRI can keep the poison pill permanently without facing broader shareholder pressure.

Shares are attractive given upside optionality

CRI does have financial flexibility to support shareholders beyond its 3.4% dividend yield. The company has $338 million of cash against $500 million of debt and $626 million of operating leases. Due to buybacks last year, its share count was down 1% from last year. With outside pressure, there could be some renewed repurchase activity. So far this year, it has burnt cash with -$35 million of free cash flow, primarily due to working capital builds. This is about $100 million worse than a year ago, and I expect H2 free cash flow to recover significantly.

Even assuming ~3% of structural margin compression from tariffs, CRI should be a ~6% margin business as it recoups some tariff costs with about $80-100 million in free cash flow. That gives shares an 8-9% free cash flow, which is generally around the fair value I see for apparel-oriented retailers. That said, I do see more upside than downside catalysts, given its cash position, high short interest, and RWWM buying.

I see shares being able to recover into the $30-33 range. CRI has challenged fundamentals, but the stock is pretty fully reflecting this, and there are potential upside catalysts if RWWM launches a proxy fight to remove the poison pill or CRI increases buybacks to alleviate shareholder pressure. As such, I see shares as a “buy” and view the stock as due to bounce despite ongoing challenges.
